sections.heading: provide a suitable framework to assist in carrying out cyber resilience assessments.
sections.title: Framework for cyber resilience assessments
sections.summary: This section requires organisations to establish a framework that supports conducting cyber resilience assessments. The framework must be practical and applicable to the organisation's specific circumstances without prescribing detailed methods. It emphasizes the need for a tailored approach rather than a one-size-fits-all solution.

requirements.text: Provide a suitable framework to assist in carrying out cyber resilience assessments

sections.source_quote: provide a suitable framework to assist in carrying out cyber resilience assessments.
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber resilience assessments directly impact data security and cross-border compliance

sections.heading: maintain the outcome-focused approach of the NCSC cyber security and resilience
sections.title: Outcome-focused cyber security assessments
sections.summary: This section mandates that cyber security assessments must focus on outcomes rather than compliance with checklists. It prohibits assessments from being conducted as mere tick-box exercises, requiring instead a practical evaluation of the organisation's actual security posture and resilience capabilities.

requirements.text: Maintain the outcome-focused approach of the NCSC cyber security and resilience principles
Discourage assessments being carried out as tick-box exercises

sections.source_quote: maintain the outcome-focused approach of the NCSC cyber security and resilience principles and discourage assessments being carried out as tick-box exercises.
sections.relevance_reason: Outcome-focused security assessments directly impact data protection and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: be compatible with the use of appropriate existing cyber security guidance and
sections.title: Compatibility with existing cyber security guidance
sections.summary: This section requires that the framework must be compatible with existing cyber security guidance and standards. It ensures that organisations can leverage established best practices and frameworks without needing to develop entirely new approaches, promoting consistency and interoperability with current industry practices.

requirements.text: Be compatible with the use of appropriate existing cyber security guidance and standards

sections.source_quote: be compatible with the use of appropriate existing cyber security guidance and standards.

sections.heading: enable the identification of effective cyber security and resilience improvement
sections.title: Identification of effective cyber resilience improvements
sections.summary: This section requires the framework to enable the identification of effective cyber security and resilience improvement activities. It focuses on practical steps that organisations can take to enhance their security posture, ensuring that improvements are meaningful and contribute to overall resilience.

requirements.text: Enable the identification of effective cyber security and resilience improvement activities

sections.source_quote: enable the identification of effective cyber security and resilience improvement activities.
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber resilience improvements directly impact data security and cross-border compliance

sections.heading: be as straightforward and cost-effective to apply as possible.
sections.title: Cost-effective application of framework
sections.summary: This section requires the framework to be as straightforward and cost-effective to apply as possible. It emphasizes practicality and affordability, ensuring that organisations can implement the framework without excessive complexity or financial burden, particularly important for resource-constrained entities.

requirements.text: Be as straightforward and cost-effective to apply as possible

sections.source_quote: be as straightforward and cost-effective to apply as possible.

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is
sections.title: Outcome-focused cyber security assessments
sections.summary: This section mandates that cyber security assessments must focus on outcomes rather than compliance with checklists. It prohibits assessments from being conducted as mere tick-box exercises, requiring instead a practical evaluation of the organisation's actual security posture and resilience capabilities.

requirements.text: Maintain the outcome-focused approach of the NCSC cyber security and resilience principles
Discourage assessments being carried out as tick-box exercises

sections.source_quote: maintain the outcome-focused approach of the NCSC cyber security and resilience principles and discourage assessments being carried out as tick-box exercises.
sections.relevance_reason: Outcome-focused security assessments directly impact data protection and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Organisation roles and decision-making for essential function security
sections.summary: Organisations must establish clear roles and responsibilities for network and information system security at all levels, with defined escalation channels for risk communication. Decision-makers must have senior-level accountability, understand the organisation's risk appetite, and delegate risk management authority effectively. Risk decisions must be made in a coordinated manner across the organisation to ensure timely and appropriate responses to threats impacting essential functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to define clear security roles and decision-making processes that prevent siloed risk management. For DTIAs, it mandates that cross-border data transfers must not compromise these governance structures, ensuring risk decisions are made with appropriate authority and visibility.

requirements.text: Organisations must establish clear roles and responsibilities for network and information system security at all levels
Organisations must define clear channels for communicating and escalating risks
Decision-makers must have senior-level accountability for security of network and information systems
Decision-makers must understand the organisation's risk appetite
Risk management decisions must be delegated and escalated effectively across the organisation
Risk management decisions must be reviewed regularly to ensure continued relevance

sections.source_quote: Your organisation has established roles and responsibilities for the security of network and information systems at all levels, with clear and well-understood channels for communicating and escalating risks.
sections.relevance_reason: Core governance for security and risk management

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Asset management and supply chain security for essential functions
sections.summary: Organisations must maintain up-to-date inventories of all assets relevant to essential functions, including dependencies between systems and infrastructure. They must prioritise assets based on operational importance and assign responsibility for managing assets throughout their lifecycle. Supply chain risks must be actively managed through understanding supplier capabilities, contractual obligations, and incident response coordination.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to manage assets and supply chains securely, which impacts DTIAs by mandating that third-party data transfers must not introduce unmanaged dependencies or insecure supply chains that could compromise essential functions.

requirements.text: Organisations must maintain up-to-date inventories of all assets relevant to essential functions
Organisations must prioritise assets based on operational importance
Organisations must assign responsibility for managing all assets throughout their lifecycle
Organisations must understand and manage supply chain risks to essential functions
Organisations must ensure suppliers demonstrate appropriate cyber security within the context of capable threat actors
Organisations must have confidence that supplier-held information is protected from capable threat actors

sections.source_quote: Everything required to deliver, maintain or support network and information systems necessary for the operation of essential functions is determined and understood.
sections.relevance_reason: Supply chain security for essential functions

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Identity and access control for essential functions
sections.summary: Organisations must define, implement, communicate and enforce security policies, processes and procedures to protect essential functions from cyber attacks. These policies must be practical, usable and appropriate for mitigating risks to network and information systems. The policies must be reviewed regularly and updated following major incidents or significant changes to technology or regulatory frameworks. The security governance approach must be integrated into all policies and procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to maintain robust identity and access controls for essential functions, including regular reviews of access rights and privileges. These controls are critical for DTIAs as they directly impact how data is protected during cross-border transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Organisations must define, implement, communicate and enforce security policies, processes and procedures for essential functions
Policies must be practical, usable and appropriate for mitigating risks to network and information systems
Policies must be reviewed regularly and updated following major incidents or significant changes
Security governance must be integrated into all policies and procedures

sections.source_quote: Principle B1 Service Protection Policies, Processes and Procedures: The organisation defines, implements, communicates and enforces appropriate policies, processes and procedures that direct its overall approach to securing systems and data that support operation of essential functions.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection and cyber-security duties

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Identity and access management for essential functions
sections.summary: Organisations must implement robust identity verification, authentication and authorisation mechanisms for essential functions. This includes using multi-factor authentication for privileged access, regularly reviewing access lists, and ensuring devices used for access are securely managed. Privileged user access must be closely managed with strong authentication and regular reviews of access rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates strong identity and access controls that are essential for DTIAs, particularly when transferring data across borders. It requires organisations to ensure that access to critical data is properly managed to prevent unauthorized access during international data transfers.

requirements.text: Organisations must use multi-factor authentication for privileged access to essential functions
Organisations must review access lists at least annually
Organisations must ensure devices used for access are securely managed
Organisations must closely manage privileged user access with strong authentication

sections.source_quote: You use additional strong authentication mechanisms, such as multi-factor authentication (MFA), for privileged access to all network and information systems that operate or support your essential function(s).
sections.relevance_reason: Core identity management for data protection

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Data impact assessment for essential functions
sections.summary: Organisations must document the impact of data compromise or unavailability on essential functions, identify mobile devices holding critical data, validate impact statements annually, and ensure necessary data minimisation. They must maintain current understanding of data links, protect data in transit and storage, and ensure backups are secure and necessary.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to assess and document data impact scenarios for essential functions, ensuring data in transit and storage is protected with appropriate safeguards to maintain critical operations during cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Document impact of data compromise or unavailability on essential functions
Identify mobile devices holding data important to essential functions
Validate documented impact statements at least annually
Minimise unnecessary copies or historic data
Maintain current understanding of data links for critical data
Protect data in transit including third-party transfers
Apply technical means to protect data over non-trusted carriers
Ensure suitable alternative transmission paths for critical data
Protect stored data from unauthorised access, modification or deletion
Ensure backups are secure and necessary for essential functions

sections.source_quote: You have not clearly articulated the impact of data compromise or lack of availability. You have identified all mobile devices and media that hold data important to the operation of network and information systems supporting your essential function(s).
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection and security requirements

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Secure system design for essential functions
sections.summary: Organisations must design security into essential systems to minimise attack surfaces, ensure system resilience, and implement robust boundary defences. They must maintain secure configurations, manage systems effectively, and address vulnerabilities promptly to protect critical operations from cyber threats.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates secure system design practices that directly impact data transfer security by requiring robust technical controls to prevent breaches during cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Design security into essential systems to minimise attack surface
Ensure essential systems remain operational after vulnerability exploitation
Implement strong boundary defences for essential systems
Design simple data flows for effective security monitoring
Securely configure essential systems with consistent, minimal settings
Document and manage security configurations for essential systems
Prevent, detect and remove malware from essential systems
Track and mitigate externally exposed vulnerabilities promptly
Regularly test vulnerabilities in essential systems

sections.source_quote: Network and information systems and technology critical for the operation of essential functions are protected from cyber attack.
sections.relevance_reason: Core cyber-security for financial data systems

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Resilience preparation for essential functions
sections.summary: Organisations must prepare to restore essential functions after cyber incidents by having business continuity and disaster recovery plans that cover network and information systems. They must assess practical implementation, understand system interdependencies, and test recovery processes. This applies to entities responsible for critical functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to have tested business continuity plans for essential functions, which impacts data transfer DTIAs by mandating robust recovery capabilities for cross-border data systems.

requirements.text: Organisations must have business continuity and disaster recovery plans for network and information systems supporting essential functions
Organisations must test business continuity and disaster recovery plans for practicality, effectiveness and completeness
Organisations must understand system interdependencies to restore essential functions
Organisations must identify and mitigate resource limitations affecting essential functions

sections.source_quote: You have business continuity and disaster recovery plans that have been tested for practicality, effectiveness and completeness. Appropriate use is made of different test methods (e.g. manual fail-over, table-top exercises, or red-teaming).
sections.relevance_reason: Resilience for critical functions impacts data security and continuity

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Cyber security training and awareness
sections.summary: Organisations must provide appropriate cyber security training for all staff, including tracking and refreshing training. They must ensure training is effective and not just measured by reach. This applies to entities handling essential functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to have effective cyber security training for staff, which impacts DTIAs by ensuring personnel are trained to handle cross-border data securely.

requirements.text: Organisations must define appropriate cyber security training for all roles from executives to junior staff
Organisations must track and refresh individual cyber security training at suitable intervals
Organisations must evaluate cyber security training to ensure it reaches the widest audience effectively

sections.source_quote: All people in your organisation, from the most senior to the most junior, follow appropriate cyber security training paths. Each individual's cyber security training is tracked and refreshed at suitable intervals.
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber security training directly impacts data protection and breach prevention

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Triage of security alerts and incident response
sections.summary: Organisations must contextualise security alerts using threat knowledge and system understanding to identify incidents and respond appropriately. They must categorise alerts by priority and severity, maintain Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for triage, document triage activities, and ensure sufficient understanding of normal user/system behaviour. Failure to meet these criteria results in inadequate incident response capabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes baseline requirements for alert triage that impact data transfer security by mandating systematic incident response processes. Effective triage ensures timely identification of security incidents that could compromise data integrity during cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Contextualise alerts with knowledge of threat and systems to identify security incidents
Categorise alerts and incidents by type and priority/severity level
Maintain Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for triage
Document triage activities performed by monitoring personnel
Ensure sufficient understanding of normal user or system behaviour for effective triage decisions

sections.source_quote: You contextualise alerts with knowledge of the threat and your systems, to identify security incidents as well as responding to all alerts appropriately.
sections.relevance_reason: Incident response directly impacts data protection and breach handling

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Threat hunting and incident response planning
sections.summary: Organisations must proactively detect adverse activity that evades standard security solutions through threat hunting. They must maintain documented threat hunting processes, record post-hunt analysis, and ensure incident response plans cover essential functions with tested recovery capabilities. These requirements ensure timely containment of security incidents affecting critical operations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates proactive threat detection and incident response planning that directly impacts data transfer security by requiring organisations to identify and mitigate threats before they compromise cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Proactively detect adverse activity affecting essential functions even when evading standard security solutions
Document threat hunting processes and post-hunt analysis
Maintain incident response plans grounded in risk assessments covering essential functions
Ensure incident response plans are tested and updated regularly
Implement back-up mechanisms to maintain essential function operations during incidents

sections.source_quote: The organisation proactively seeks to detect, within networks and information systems, adverse activity affecting, or with the potential to affect, the operation of essential functions even when the activity evades standard security prevent / detect solutions
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber incident response for critical functions

sections.heading: using the relevant IGPs, the circumstances under which the contributing outcome is (cont.)
sections.title: Incident lessons and improvements
sections.summary: Organisations must conduct comprehensive post-incident analysis to identify root causes and implement remediation actions. They must use lessons learned to improve security measures, including updating response plans, prioritising improvements, and incorporating findings into risk management. This ensures continuous improvement in incident response capabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires organisations to systematically improve security measures after incidents, which directly impacts data transfer security by ensuring vulnerabilities in cross-border data flows are addressed through continuous improvement.

requirements.text: Conduct comprehensive post-incident analysis to identify root causes and contributing factors
Implement security improvements based on lessons learned from incidents
Prioritise and promptly implement high-priority security improvements
Incorporate lessons learned into risk management and continuous improvement processes

sections.source_quote: When an incident occurs, steps are taken to understand its causes and to ensure remediating action is taken to protect against future incidents.
sections.relevance_reason: Incident response and security improvements directly impact data protection
